content.text: What it is

Ensemble review for prompt and tool changes is the practice of requiring multiple specialist reviewers — including product, safety, and model behaviour expertise — to approve any pull request that modifies a system prompt, few-shot example set, tool schema, or agent orchestration logic. Unlike standard code review, which typically requires one or two engineers from the owning team, ensemble review assembles a cross-functional group whose combined expertise covers the full risk surface of an AI-facing change.

The practice is modelled on ensemble programming (mob programming applied to review), where the group collectively inspects the change rather than passing it through sequential individual reviewers. In practice, ensemble review for prompt changes often runs asynchronously via pull request, but retains the ensemble principle: no single reviewer has sufficient expertise to approve the change alone, and the PR template enforces that each specialist role has signed off before the change can merge.

attrs.alt: Ensemble Review for Prompt Changes — review flow with four reviewer types

content.text: What Ensemble Review Means for Prompt Changes

Ensemble review for prompt changes means that every proposed change to the approved system prompt is reviewed by multiple stakeholders with distinct perspectives before it is merged. The term 'ensemble' distinguishes this from a standard code review where one technical reviewer approves. For prompt changes, the review requires at least two different types of expertise: safety and product.

The need for ensemble review arises from the unique nature of system prompt changes. A single-word change in the system prompt can produce materially different agent behaviour across thousands of user sessions. A standard code review process, where one senior developer approves a change, is not adequate for this level of risk. The ensemble review ensures that at least a Safety Reviewer and a Product Manager have independently confirmed that the change is safe and aligned with the approved design.

Ensemble review is not bureaucratic slowdown — it is a quality gate calibrated to the risk level of the artefact being changed. The review SLA for Minor changes is four business hours. A well-functioning ensemble review process should not block engineering velocity.
The Required Reviewers
Safety Reviewer (mandatory for all changes):

reviews the harm category impact, verifies that refusal phrasing is accurate, checks that HITL thresholds have not been weakened, and confirms injection resistance is intact. The Safety Reviewer must approve before the PM reviews — their approval is a prerequisite, not a parallel track.
Product Manager (mandatory for all changes):

reviews whether the change aligns with the approved PRD scope, confirms the change classification is correct, verifies the change log entry exists, and assesses the eval delta against the stated motivation. The PM approves after the Safety Reviewer.
Engineering Lead (required for Major and Safety class):

reviews the technical impact — context window budget, token count change, new tool call integration, and streaming behaviour compatibility. The Engineering Lead reviews in parallel with the Safety Reviewer but must approve before merge.
Automated CI checks (always run):

the CI pipeline validates the prompt hash matches the file, verifies the change log entry references the new hash, checks for secrets in the prompt file, and runs the full eval suite. CI must pass before human reviewers are requested.
The PR Template

Every system prompt change PR must use this template. The template enforces the information that reviewers need to evaluate the change efficiently. PRs without this information should be returned to the author before review begins:

## Prompt Change PR Template
### Change Classification
- [ ] Minor (wording only, no semantic shift) — PM approval required
- [ ] Major (scope, tone, tool, or failure path change) — PM + Safety approval required
- [ ] Safety (harm category, refusal, HITL, or guardrail change) — Safety + PM + Eng Lead required
- [ ] Emergency (active harm; retroactive approval) — Eng Lead + Safety required

### Prompt Diff Summary
Previous behaviour:
  [Describe what the agent did before this change — one sentence per affected behaviour]

New behaviour:
  [Describe what the agent does after this change — one sentence per affected behaviour]

### Hash
  Previous approved hash : [8 chars]
  New prompt hash        : [8 chars]
  Verification command   : sha256sum prompts/approved/<new-hash>.txt | cut -c1-8

### Motivation
  [Why was this change needed? What failure mode or user feedback drove it?]

### Eval Suite Results
  Before this change: [N] passing / [N] failing
  After this change:  [N] passing / [N] failing
  New eval cases added: [N] covering [which failure modes]
  Command: python run_evals.py --feature [feature-id] --prompt prompts/approved/<new-hash>.txt

### Red-Team Results (required for Safety class changes)
  Attach output of: python red_team.py --feature [id] --prompt prompts/approved/<new-hash>.txt
  Pass rate: [N]/[N] tests passed

### Change Log Entry
  [ ] Entry added to design-change-log.md before this PR was created
  Entry reference: [link or entry number]

### Reviewer Checklist
  Safety Reviewer:
  [ ] Harm categories not weakened by this change
  [ ] Refusal conditions accurate
  [ ] HITL thresholds unchanged (or change is explicitly intended and approved)
  [ ] Injection resistance intact
  [ ] Red-team results attached and satisfactory

  PM Reviewer:
  [ ] Change matches approved PRD scope
  [ ] Change classification is correct
  [ ] Change log entry verified
  [ ] Eval delta is acceptable for the stated motivation

### Approval Required
  Safety Reviewer : @[username]
  PM              : @[username]
  Eng Lead        : @[username] (required for Major and Safety class only)
Automated Pre-Review Checks
Before requesting human review, the CI pipeline runs these automated checks. This script is integrated into the CI system and runs on every PR that modifies a file in the `/prompts/` directory:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# prompt_pr_check.py
# CI script that validates a prompt change PR before review is requested.
# Exits 0 if all automated checks pass, 1 otherwise.
# Usage: python prompt_pr_check.py --feature billing-agent --old-hash a3f8c2d1 --new-hash b7e901cc

import argparse
import hashlib
import json
import os
import re
import subprocess
import sys

def compute_hash(path: str) -> str:
    if not os.path.exists(path):
        return ""
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        return hashlib.sha256(f.read()).hexdigest()[:8]

def check_hash_matches_file(prompt_dir: str, new_hash: str) -> bool:
    prompt_file = os.path.join(prompt_dir, f"{new_hash}.txt")
    if not os.path.exists(prompt_file):
        print(f"  FAIL: Prompt file not found: {prompt_file}")
        return False
    actual_hash = compute_hash(prompt_file)
    if actual_hash != new_hash:
        print(f"  FAIL: Hash mismatch. File hashes to {actual_hash}, PR says {new_hash}")
        return False
    print(f"  OK: Prompt file hash verified: {new_hash}")
    return True

def check_changelog_entry(changelog_path: str, new_hash: str) -> bool:
    if not os.path.exists(changelog_path):
        print(f"  FAIL: Change log not found: {changelog_path}")
        return False
    with open(changelog_path) as f:
        content = f.read()
    if new_hash not in content:
        print(f"  FAIL: New hash {new_hash} not found in change log")
        return False
    print(f"  OK: Change log entry found for hash {new_hash}")
    return True

def check_no_secrets(prompt_dir: str, new_hash: str) -> bool:
    prompt_file = os.path.join(prompt_dir, f"{new_hash}.txt")
    if not os.path.exists(prompt_file):
        return False
    with open(prompt_file) as f:
        content = f.read()
    secret_patterns = [
        r"sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{32,}",   # OpenAI API key pattern
        r"AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}",       # AWS access key
        r"[a-z0-9]{32}-[a-z0-9]{8}",  # Generic token pattern
    ]
    for pattern in secret_patterns:
        if re.search(pattern, content):
            print(f"  FAIL: Potential secret detected in prompt file")
            return False
    print(f"  OK: No secrets detected in prompt file")
    return True

def run_eval_suite(feature_id: str, prompt_path: str) -> bool:
    if not os.path.exists("run_evals.py"):
        print("  SKIP: run_evals.py not found (eval suite check skipped)")
        return True
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "run_evals.py", "--feature", feature_id, "--prompt", prompt_path],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"  FAIL: Eval suite failed:\n{result.stdout[-500:]}")
        return False
    print(f"  OK: Eval suite passed")
    return True

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--feature",    required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--old-hash",   required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--new-hash",   required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--prompt-dir", default="prompts/approved")
    parser.add_argument("--changelog",  default="design-change-log.md")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(f"\nPrompt PR Check: {args.feature}\n" + "-" * 40)
    print(f"  Old hash: {args.old_hash}  ->  New hash: {args.new_hash}")

    checks = [
        check_hash_matches_file(args.prompt_dir, args.new_hash),
        check_changelog_entry(args.changelog, args.new_hash),
        check_no_secrets(args.prompt_dir, args.new_hash),
        run_eval_suite(args.feature,
                       os.path.join(args.prompt_dir, f"{args.new_hash}.txt")),
    ]

    print()
    if all(checks):
        print("AUTOMATED CHECKS PASSED. Ready for human review.")
        sys.exit(0)
    else:
        failed = sum(1 for c in checks if not c)
        print(f"AUTOMATED CHECKS FAILED: {failed} check(s) failed. Fix before requesting review.")
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Review SLAs and Escalation
Ensemble review SLAs are calibrated to the change class. These SLAs apply from the time the PR passes all automated CI checks — not from the time the PR is opened.
Minor change:
Safety Reviewer within 4 business hours, PM within 4 business hours after Safety Reviewer approves. Total maximum elapsed time: 8 business hours.
Major change:
Safety Reviewer within 24 business hours, PM and Eng Lead within 24 business hours after Safety Reviewer approves. Total maximum elapsed time: 48 business hours.
Safety change:
Safety Reviewer within 24 business hours, PM and Eng Lead within 24 business hours. Total: 48 business hours.
Emergency change:
Eng Lead or Safety Reviewer authorises deployment within 2 hours. All other reviews collected retroactively within 24 hours.
SLA breach escalation:
if a reviewer does not respond within the SLA, the PM escalates to their manager. The escalation path must be documented in the change control policy.
What the Safety Reviewer Actually Looks For

The Safety Reviewer's review is often the most important and least understood part of the ensemble. It is not a general code review and not a spell-check. The safety reviewer is specifically looking for four things:
Does this change weaken any harm category protection? A change that makes a refusal condition more permissive, even slightly, is a safety regression regardless of the stated motivation.
Does this change affect the agent's HITL behaviour? Any change near the HITL threshold logic must be explicitly noted and verified to not lower the threshold unintentionally.
Does this change create a new injection surface? New instructions that reference content from user-supplied documents create new injection attack surfaces.
Is the red-team test result attached for Safety class changes, and does it show 100% pass? A Safety class change without a red-team result attached is incomplete and should not be reviewed.

The Safety Reviewer is not responsible for reviewing whether the change achieves its stated product goal. That is the PM's responsibility. The division of responsibilities is clean: Safety Reviewer owns safety, PM owns scope and product alignment.
CODEOWNERS Configuration

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Prompt files require both Safety and PM approval to merge.
# This is enforced at the GitHub repository level, not just by convention.

/prompts/approved/         @safety-reviewer @product-manager
/prompts/drafts/           @engineering-team
/design-change-log.md      @product-manager @safety-reviewer
/config/active-prompts.json @engineering-lead @product-manager

Configure CODEOWNERS to require both the Safety Reviewer and the Product Manager as required approvers for any file under `/prompts/approved/`. This makes the ensemble review mandatory at the repository level — it cannot be bypassed by accident or by time pressure. Neither reviewer can merge their own approval — both must approve independently.
Common Ensemble Review Failures
Parallel reviews without sequencing:
Safety Reviewer and PM reviewing simultaneously means the PM may approve a change that the Safety Reviewer has not yet evaluated. Always enforce the sequencing: Safety first, PM second, then merge.
Safety Reviewer approving without the red-team result:
for Safety class changes, the red-team result is a prerequisite for the safety review. A safety approval without the red-team result is not a valid safety approval.
PR approved without an updated change log:
the CI check must verify the change log entry before the PR can even request review. Do not allow the CI check to be bypassed.
Reviewers approving out-of-scope items:

the Safety Reviewer sometimes comments on product scope (not their domain), and the PM sometimes comments on safety (also not their domain). Clarify the boundary: each reviewer focuses on their domain only. Cross-domain concerns are noted as comments, not used as blocking approvals.
Review Metrics and Continuous Improvement

Ensemble review is only as strong as the team's visibility into how it is performing. Tracking review metrics over time reveals bottlenecks, reviewer fatigue, and process gaps before they translate into production incidents. The following script collects PR-level metrics from GitHub and produces a per-class summary report that can be posted to a team dashboard.

# review_metrics.py
# Collect and report ensemble review metrics from GitHub PR data
# Run weekly: python review_metrics.py --repo org/repo --days 30

import os
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional

try:
    import requests
except ImportError:
    raise SystemExit("pip install requests")

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
HEADERS = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}", "Accept": "application/vnd.github.v3+json"}

@dataclass
class ReviewMetrics:
    pr_number: int
    change_class: str          # minor | major | safety | emergency
    time_to_first_review: float   # hours
    time_to_merge: float          # hours
    review_cycles: int            # number of round-trips before approval
    safety_reviewer: str
    pm_reviewer: str
    ci_pass_on_first_try: bool
    reverted_within_7d: bool = False

def fetch_prompt_prs(repo: str, days: int) -> List[dict]:
    since = (datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=days)).isoformat() + "Z"
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/pulls"
    params = {"state": "closed", "sort": "updated", "direction": "desc", "per_page": 100}
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, params=params)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    prs = resp.json()
    # Filter to prompt-change PRs by label
    return [pr for pr in prs if any(l["name"] == "prompt-change" for l in pr.get("labels", []))]

def classify_change(pr: dict) -> str:
    for label in pr.get("labels", []):
        name = label["name"]
        if name.startswith("class:"):
            return name.split("class:", 1)[1]
    return "unknown"

def compute_cycle_time(pr: dict) -> tuple[float, float]:
    created_at = datetime.fromisoformat(pr["created_at"].rstrip("Z"))
    merged_at_str = pr.get("merged_at")
    if not merged_at_str:
        return 0.0, 0.0
    merged_at = datetime.fromisoformat(merged_at_str.rstrip("Z"))
    total_hours = (merged_at - created_at).total_seconds() / 3600
    # First review approximated as 30% of total for demo
    first_review_hours = total_hours * 0.3
    return first_review_hours, total_hours

def report_metrics(metrics: List[ReviewMetrics]) -> None:
    by_class = defaultdict(list)
    for m in metrics:
        by_class[m.change_class].append(m)
    print("\n=== Ensemble Review Metrics ===")
    for cls, items in sorted(by_class.items()):
        avg_ttm = sum(m.time_to_merge for m in items) / len(items)
        avg_cycles = sum(m.review_cycles for m in items) / len(items)
        ci_first = sum(1 for m in items if m.ci_pass_on_first_try) / len(items) * 100
        reverted = sum(1 for m in items if m.reverted_within_7d)
        print(f"  {cls.upper()}: {len(items)} PRs | avg merge {avg_ttm:.1f}h | avg cycles {avg_cycles:.1f} | CI pass {ci_first:.0f}% | reverted {reverted}")

def main():
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--repo", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--days", type=int, default=30)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    prs = fetch_prompt_prs(args.repo, args.days)
    results = []
    for pr in prs:
        change_class = classify_change(pr)
        ttfr, ttm = compute_cycle_time(pr)
        results.append(ReviewMetrics(
            pr_number=pr["number"],
            change_class=change_class,
            time_to_first_review=ttfr,
            time_to_merge=ttm,
            review_cycles=1,           # simplified
            safety_reviewer="unknown",
            pm_reviewer="unknown",
            ci_pass_on_first_try=True, # simplified
        ))
    report_metrics(results)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Run this script weekly and post the output to a shared Slack channel or wiki page. The key metrics to watch are average merge time by change class, the number of review cycles before approval, the CI-pass-on-first-try rate, and the revert rate within seven days of deployment. An increasing revert rate is the strongest signal that the ensemble review process is not catching real issues.
Improving the Review Process Over Time
Metrics alone are not enough — the team must act on them. The following improvement patterns address the most common failure modes that emerge as ensemble review scales.
Track time-to-first-review per reviewer role weekly. A rising trend indicates review fatigue or understaffing in that role — reassign or rotate before it becomes a bottleneck.
Track CI-pass-on-first-try rate. Below 80% suggests developers are not running the pre-commit hook locally — add a Makefile target `make prompt-check` that runs prompt_pr_check.py before pushing.

Correlate review cycles with post-deploy incidents. PRs that needed three or more cycles before merge are higher incident predictors — use this to triage which changes need extra production monitoring.

Run a quarterly ensemble review retrospective. Ask each reviewer role: what information was missing, what slowed you down, what false positives did you block? Use findings to update the PR template and CODEOWNERS.
Publish a monthly ensemble review health dashboard to the team. Transparency about SLA compliance, reviewer load, and revert rates creates accountability without requiring management escalation.
When to Escalate

Ensemble review assumes all required reviewers are available and willing to approve. In practice, reviewers are sometimes unavailable, deadlines create pressure to bypass checks, or reviewers disagree. The following escalation rules define what to do in each case.
The Safety Reviewer is unavailable for a Minor change: wait for the next available Safety Reviewer. Do not bypass — Minor changes still touch system prompts and require safety eyes.
Safety and PM reviewers disagree and cannot converge: escalate to the Head of AI Product within 24 hours. Do not merge until the disagreement is resolved in writing.
A PR has been in review for longer than double the SLA: the PR author should post a status comment and Slack-ping the blocked reviewer directly. If still unresolved, escalate to the Eng Lead.
A change is classified as Major but the author believes Minor classification is justified: the Safety Reviewer makes the final classification decision. Authors cannot self-downgrade classification.

CI fails on the eval suite with a regression that seems spurious: the Safety Reviewer and one engineer must both independently confirm the regression is spurious before bypassing CI. Document the bypass in the PR.

Escalation rules must be written down and linked from the PR template. If they exist only as informal team knowledge, they will be applied inconsistently under pressure. Add a link to the escalation playbook in the PR template's 'Reviewer Checklist' section so it is visible at review time.
Benefits
Cross-functional coverage: each specialist reviewer checks for failure modes outside other reviewers' expertise, reducing the chance that any single risk vector is missed.
Knowledge transfer: ensemble review exposes engineers to how prompt changes are evaluated by safety and product roles, gradually raising the team's collective review capability.
Audit trail: a merged PR with explicit sign-offs from safety, product, and a second engineer provides a compliance record that the change was reviewed by qualified parties.
Earlier defect detection: prompt issues found in review are far cheaper to fix than issues found in production through customer-reported incidents.
Trade-offs

Ensemble review adds latency to the change pipeline. A prompt change that previously required one reviewer and could merge within hours now requires coordinating availability across three or more specialist roles, which adds cycle time, particularly when reviewers are in different time zones. Teams must decide which changes are high-stakes enough to justify ensemble review versus which can use standard single-reviewer approval.
Review latency: ensemble reviews add 1–3 business days of cycle time; triage changes by risk tier to apply ensemble review only where warranted.
Reviewer bottleneck: specialist safety reviewers are scarce; large teams may need to train a broader group to prevent review queue backlog.
Review fatigue: too many PRs routed to ensemble review desensitises reviewers; calibrate the risk threshold to keep ensemble review volume manageable.
When to use it
System prompt changes that add, remove, or alter behavioural constraints (safety filters, scope restrictions, refusal criteria).
Few-shot example changes that could shift the model's output distribution toward or away from safe behaviour patterns.
Tool schema changes that add write-capable tools, remove safety checks, or alter the arguments accepted by sensitive operations.
Changes to orchestration logic that alter which tools are called when, potentially enabling new capability pathways.
Best practices

Maintain an explicit risk classification for prompt and tool changes: Low (cosmetic wording), Medium (scope or persona), High (safety constraints, write tools). Apply ensemble review at Medium and above.
Pre-populate the PR template with context automatically: use a CI step that generates a semantic diff of the prompt change and attaches it to the PR body, reducing the burden on the change author.
Rotate safety reviewer duty across a pool of trained engineers to prevent a single-person bottleneck and build organisational safety review capacity.

Document review decisions, not just approvals: require each reviewer to leave a comment stating what they checked and any concerns they resolved, creating a richer audit trail than a bare approval click.
Tooling
Tool
Role in ensemble review
Notes
GitHub CODEOWNERS
Automatically requests required reviewers based on file path
Prompt files mapped to safety team; tool schemas to platform team
Branch protection rules
Enforce required reviewers and PR checks before merge
Set required_approving_review_count to 2 for prompt paths
Semantic diff script
Generates human-readable prompt diff attached to PR body
Highlights added/removed constraints, example count changes
Eval regression gate
Runs eval suite against changed prompt before review
Provides safety reviewer with quantitative quality signal
Key takeaways
Ensemble review applies the mob programming principle to code review: no single reviewer can approve a high-risk prompt or tool change unilaterally.
Required reviewers (safety, product, second engineer) are enforced by CODEOWNERS and branch protection rules, not by convention alone.
Automated pre-review checks (prompt diff, eval regression gate, schema validation) give reviewers the context they need to review efficiently.
Review SLAs and escalation paths prevent ensemble review from becoming a bottleneck while maintaining the cross-functional sign-off requirement.
Apply ensemble review selectively based on risk classification: not all prompt changes are equal, and blanket application creates review fatigue without proportionate safety benefit.
